title: Ember: An Extensible Benchmark Suite for Quantum Annealing Embedding Algorithms
authors: Zachary Macaskill-Smith, Unmol Sharma, Melissa Warner, Kálmán Varga, David A. B. Hyde
abstract: Minor embedding is a required compilation step for quantum annealing, mapping logical problem graphs onto sparse hardware topologies. Despite its central role in determining solution quality, no standardized benchmark exists for comparing embedding algorithms: prior studies use incompatible graph libraries, inconsistent metrics, and non-reproducible experimental setups, making cross-algorithm comparisons unreliable. We present Ember (Embedding Minor Benchmark for Evaluative Reproducibility), an open-source benchmarking framework addressing this gap. Ember provides a standardized algorithm interface with seeded, reproducible execution infrastructure; a diverse graph library of 24,016 instances spanning structured, random, and physics-motivated problem types not previously used in embedding benchmarks; and a unified analysis pipeline supporting all three current D-Wave hardware topologies (Chimera, Pegasus, Zephyr). We evaluate five algorithms across the full library on Chimera and find that no algorithm dominates universally: rankings vary systematically with graph structure, and the best algorithm depends on the family being embedded. We also examine the effects of hardware topology (including Pegasus and Zephyr), qubit error rates, and evaluate a reinforcement-learning approach (CHARME) within a narrower test set. Ember is available at https://github.com/zachmacsmith/ember and is installable via pip install ember-qc.
summary: This paper introduces Ember, a new benchmarking suite designed to provide a standardized way to compare algorithms that map logical problems to quantum annealing hardware. By testing a vast library of graph types, the authors demonstrate that the 'best' algorithm depends heavily on the specific structure of the problem and the hardware topology being used. The framework also allows researchers to study how embedding quality degrades under realistic hardware errors. This work is crucial for the development of more efficient compilers for quantum annealing processors.
main_problem: The lack of a standardized, reproducible, and comparable benchmarking framework for minor embedding algorithms used to map logical problem graphs onto the sparse hardware topologies of quantum annealers.
main_result: The authors introduce Ember, a benchmarking suite that reveals no single embedding algorithm dominates universally; performance rankings vary systematically based on graph structure, hardware topology (Chimera, Pegasus, Zephyr), and hardware fault rates.
method: The development of Ember, an open-source Python framework featuring a massive library of 24,016 graphs across 35 categories and a unified analysis pipeline to evaluate algorithms like MINORMINER, OCT-FAST, and CHARME.
model_or_system: The study focuses on D-Wave quantum annealing hardware architectures, specifically the Chimera, Pegasus, and Zephyr topologies, used for solving Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problems.
key_observables: Success rate, Average Chain Length (ACL), Maximum Chain Length (MCL), wall time (runtime), and algorithmic retention under simulated hardware faults.
important_parameters: Graph node count (up to 65,536), edge density, hardware connectivity (node degree), and qubit/edge error rates (fault rates up to 0.25).
main_assumptions: Chain length is used as a proxy for annealing fidelity; the fault model assumes uniform random removal of qubits/edges rather than spatially correlated errors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows success rate decay vs. node count; Figure 2 plots Mean ACL vs. edge count; Figure 3 illustrates algorithmic retention under increasing hardware fault rates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of embedding standardization, presents the Ember framework and its graph library, details the evaluation of various algorithms across different topologies, analyzes performance scaling and robustness to faults, and concludes with future research directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Success rate vs. node count (20-node bins, all graph categories pooled). Shaded bands show pointwise 95% confidence intervals (Wilson score interval for binomial proportions, computed per 20-node bin). CLIQUE fails completely above n = 64—the maximum clique K64 embeddable in Chimera C16—producing a 100% →0% transition in a single step. Above 80 nodes, only MINORMINER is maintaining meaningful success rates.

Fig. 3. Baseline-conditioned retention stratified by source edge count: fraction of embeddings successful at f = 0 that remain successful at each fault rate f. MINORMINER degrades gracefully across all graph sizes; PSSA and CLIQUE exhibit sharp cliffs between f = 0.01 and f = 0.05 at medium edge counts. The 1001–3000 row for PSSA and CLIQUE is sparsely populated (few baseline successes) and its precision is correspondingly limited; the > 3000 row has no baseline successes for any algorithm.
